title: Chapter 3 Quiz
description: Test your logic! Prove you understand if, unless, and case statements.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an `if` statement?
options: To run a piece of code repeatedly.
To check a condition and only execute a block of code if the condition is true.
questions.explanation: If statements represent 'Control Flow'. They decide whether code should run or be bypassed entirely.

questions.question: Which keyword is required at the bottom to close out an `if` block?
questions.explanation: Ruby uses `end` to define where a block of code completes.

questions.question: When does the code inside an `else` block run?
options: Only when the initial `if` condition evaluates to false.
Only when the initial `if` condition evaluates to true.
Every single time the program runs.
questions.explanation: `else` acts as the safety net/backup plan. It only runs if the first condition fails.

questions.question: How do you check for multiple, separate conditions in a row before falling back to `else`?
questions.explanation: Ruby spells it `elsif` (without the middle 'e').

questions.question: In a block with multiple `elsif` conditions, if several of them are 'true', Ruby will run all of them.
questions.explanation: Ruby evaluates them top-to-bottom. It will run the VERY FIRST one that is true, and then immediately skip the rest.

questions.question: What does the `unless` keyword do?
options: It acts identical to a while loop.
It runs the code only if the condition evaluates to FALSE.
It ensures code runs no matter what.
It pauses the program until a user clicks 'Next'.
questions.explanation: `unless` literally translates to 'if not'. It is the reverse of `if`.

questions.question: If we write `unless is_raining`, what is the plain English translation of that logic?
questions.explanation: Unless translates perfectly to 'If Not'.

questions.question: Which operator do you use to verify that BOTH conditions are strictly `true`?
questions.explanation: The double ampersand `&&` is the logical AND operator.

questions.question: In the statement `true && false`, what is the final result?
questions.explanation: Because `&&` demands EVERYTHING be true, a single false ruins it all.

questions.question: Which operator do you use to verify that at least ONE of the conditions is `true`?
questions.explanation: The double pipe `||` represents logical OR.

questions.question: In the statement `true || false`, what is the final result?
questions.explanation: Because `||` (OR) only needs ONE side to be true, it evaluates to true overall.

questions.question: The exclamation mark (`!`) operator flips a boolean to its exact opposite. It's often called the _____ operator.
questions.explanation: The `!` acts as a NOT. So `!true` means NOT true (which is false).

questions.question: Ruby allows you to place `if` statements on the exact same line as the action, like this: `puts "Hi" if true`
questions.explanation: Yes, this is called a single-line conditional and is highly encouraged in Ruby when appropriate.

questions.question: When is a `case` statement usually preferred over `if` statements?
options: When checking if a number is greater than another number.
When you only have a single binary Yes/No decision to make.
When you need to test a single variable against many different possible matches.
questions.explanation: Case statements clean up the visual clutter of having ten different `elsif` chains.

questions.question: Which keyword sits natively inside a `case` statement block to define the individual potential matches?
questions.explanation: A case statement uses `case [variable]` followed by one or more `when [option]` checks.

questions.question: What is the result of `!(5 == 5)`?
questions.explanation: `5 == 5` is true. The `!` operator flips that `true` to `false`.

questions.question: What is "Short Circuiting" in Ruby?
options: When your computer battery drops below 10%.
A bug that crashes infinite loops.
When Ruby stops reading an `||` logic string early because it already found a `true`.
When you use too many `elsif` statements.
questions.explanation: If the left side of an OR `||` is true, Ruby doesn't even bother processing the right side because it knows the answer is already ultimately true.

questions.question: You should regularly chain an `else` block onto an `unless` block.
questions.explanation: While Ruby allows it, using `unless / else` is highly confusing to read. Stick to `if / else`.

questions.question: To provide a catch-all fallback at the very bottom of a `case` statement, you can use an _____ block.
questions.explanation: Just like an `if` statement, an `else` at the bottom of a `case` statement runs if no `when` clauses triggered.

questions.question: If you want to keep your Case Statement extremely compact, you can use the _____ keyword to put everything on one line.
questions.explanation: You can use the `then` keyword to write it like `when "A" then puts "Apple"`.
